subtopics.title: Components
subtopics.content:
- **136 tiles** total:
  - **Suited tiles (108)**: 3 suits (Characters/Manzu, Circles/Pinzu, Bamboo/Souzu) × 9 ranks × 4 copies each
  - **Honor tiles (28)**: 4 Wind tiles (East, South, West, North) × 4 copies + 3 Dragon tiles (White, Green, Red) × 4 copies
- **Scoring sticks (tenbo)**: 10,000-point sticks, 5,000-point sticks, 1,000-point sticks, 100-point sticks
- **Dice** (2)
- **Seat wind marker** and **round wind marker**
- **Riichi sticks** (1,000-point sticks used for declaring riichi)

subtopics.title: Table Setup
subtopics.content:
1. **Build walls** — Shuffle all 136 tiles face down. Each player builds a wall 17 tiles long and 2 tiles high.
2. **Determine dealer (East)** — Roll dice, count players counterclockwise to find first East.
3. **Break wall** — Dealer rolls dice, counts from right end of their wall, and breaks the wall at that point.
4. **Deal tiles** — Starting from the break, deal 4 tiles at a time counterclockwise until each player has 12, then 1 more each (dealer gets 14, others get 13).
5. **Set dead wall** — Separate 14 tiles (7 stacks) from the end of the wall as the dead wall.
6. **Flip dora indicator** — Flip the 3rd tile from the end of the dead wall face up.
7. **Starting points** — Each player starts with 25,000 points.

subtopics.title: Player Setup
subtopics.content:
- **Sort hand** — Arrange your 13 tiles (hidden from others).
- **Seat winds** — Players are assigned East, South, West, North winds based on seating.
- **Dealer (East)** — Takes the first turn. Starts with 14 tiles.
- **Round wind** — Starts as East Round.

subtopics.title: Game Overview
subtopics.content: Riichi Mahjong is a 4-player tile game. Players draw and discard tiles to complete a winning hand of 14 tiles — typically 4 sets (melds) plus 1 pair. Hands are scored based on which scoring patterns (yaku) they contain. A full game consists of at least an East Round and a South Round (8 hands minimum).

subtopics.title: Turn Structure
subtopics.content:
Play proceeds counterclockwise:
1. **Draw** — Draw 1 tile from the wall (or claim a discard — see below).
2. **Optional: Declare Kan** — If you have 4 of the same tile, you may declare a kan (quad).
3. **Discard** — Place 1 tile face up in your discard area.

#### Claiming Discards
When any player discards, others may claim it (priority order):
- **Ron (win)** — Highest priority. Claim the discard to complete your winning hand.
- **Pon** — Claim to make a triplet (3 of a kind). Any player may pon.
- **Chi** — Claim to make a sequence (3 consecutive suited tiles). Only the player to the right (next in turn) may chi.
- **Claiming interrupts** normal turn order. After claiming, the claimer discards and play continues from them.

subtopics.title: Key Actions
subtopics.content:
#### Winning Hand Structure
- **Standard**: 4 melds + 1 pair (14 tiles total).
- **Melds**: Sequences (3 consecutive same-suit) or Triplets (3 identical tiles).
- **Must have yaku** — A winning hand must contain at least 1 scoring pattern (yaku).

#### Tsumo (Self-draw Win)
- Complete your hand by drawing from the wall. All other players pay.

#### Ron (Discard Win)
- Complete your hand using another player's discard. That player pays full value.

#### Riichi Declaration
- When your hand is tenpai (1 tile from winning) and fully closed (no claimed tiles), you may declare riichi.
- Place a 1,000-point stick on the table.
- You cannot change your hand after declaring (only discard drawn tiles).
- Riichi itself is a yaku worth 1 han.

#### Kan (Quad)
- **Closed kan** — 4 of the same tile in hand, all concealed.
- **Open kan** — Add a drawn tile to an existing open triplet.
- **Added kan** — Add the 4th tile to your own open pon.
- After any kan, draw a replacement tile from the dead wall and flip another dora indicator.

#### Dora
- The flipped dora indicator shows a tile; the next tile in sequence is the dora.
- Each dora in a winning hand adds 1 han to the score (but dora alone is not a yaku).

subtopics.title: Special Rules
subtopics.content:
#### Furiten
- If any of your winning tiles is in your own discard pile, you cannot win by ron (only tsumo).
- Temporary furiten: if you pass on a ron opportunity, you cannot ron until your next turn.

#### Exhaustive Draw
- If the wall runs out (14 tiles remain as dead wall), the hand is a draw.
- Tenpai players receive points from non-tenpai players (3,000 point pool).

#### Dealer Rotation
- If the dealer wins or is tenpai at a draw, they remain dealer (renchan) and a bonus counter is added.
- Otherwise, dealership passes to the next player.

#### Scoring Basics
- **Han** — Scoring multipliers from yaku and dora.
- **Fu** — Base points from hand composition and wait type.
- Points = fu × 2^(2+han), with caps at mangan (2,000/4,000 base) and above.
- **Mangan**: 3 han/70fu or 4 han/30fu+. **Haneman**: 6-7 han. **Baiman**: 8-10 han. **Sanbaiman**: 11-12 han. **Yakuman**: 13+ han.

subtopics.title: Scoring and Endgame
subtopics.content:
#### Game End
- After completing East Round and South Round (or when a player drops below 0 points).
- Player with the most points at end wins.

#### Common Yaku (Scoring Patterns)
- **Riichi** (1 han) — Declared tenpai with closed hand.
- **Tsumo** (1 han) — Win by self-draw with closed hand.
- **Pinfu** (1 han) — Closed hand, all sequences, valueless pair, two-sided wait.
- **Tanyao** (1 han) — All tiles numbered 2-8 (no terminals or honors).
- **Iipeiko** (1 han) — Two identical sequences in closed hand.
- **Yakuhai** (1 han each) — Triplet of dragons, seat wind, or round wind.
- **Toitoi** (2 han) — All triplets.
- **Honitsu** (3 han closed / 2 open) — One suit plus honors.
- **Chinitsu** (6 han closed / 5 open) — One suit only.

subtopics.title: Beginner Tips
subtopics.content:
- **Keep your hand closed** — Calling pon/chi opens your hand and limits yaku options.
- **Aim for tanyao** — All simples (2-8) is the easiest yaku to build.
- **Declare riichi when ready** — Riichi is a free yaku that also enables ippatsu and ura dora.
- **Watch discards** — Track what's been discarded to know which tiles are safe and which waits are live.
- **Defend when behind** — If an opponent declares riichi, consider folding (discarding safe tiles).

subtopics.title: Key Strategies
subtopics.content:
- **Efficiency** — Maximize tile acceptance (the number of tiles that improve your hand).
- **Push vs fold** — Decide whether to pursue your hand or play defensively based on risk.
- **Suji defense** — Use mathematical patterns to identify safer discards against riichi.
- **Point situation** — Adjust strategy in later rounds based on standings (go for big hands when behind).
- **Dealer advantage** — Dealer pays/receives 1.5× and can repeat; play more aggressively as dealer.
